concepts.title: Introduction to Continuity
concepts.conceptBody:
Continuity 

Continuity in general means that you can draw the graph of a function without lifting your pen, in its defined domain.

Continuity at a Point 

In practice, most functions of a real variable have domains that are intervals or unions of separate intervals, and it is natural to restrict our study of continuity to functions with these domains. This leaves us with only three kinds of points to consider: interior points (points lie in an open interval in the domain ), left endpoints , and right endpoints .

Definition 

A function $f$ is continuous at an interior point $x=c$ of its domain if

$\underset{x\to c}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,f\left( x \right)=f\left( c \right)$

In Fig. the first function is continuous at $x=0$. The function in (b) would be continuous if it had $\text{f}\left( 0 \right)=1$. The function in (c) would be continuous if $\text{f}\left( 0 \right)$ were 1 instead of 2. The discontinuities in (b) and (c) are removable . Each function has a limit as $x\to 0$, and we can remove the discontinuity by setting $\text{f}\left( 0 \right)$ equal to this limit.

The discontinuities in parts (d)-(f) are more serious: $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to 0}}f\left( x \right)$ does not exist and there is no way to improve the situation by changing $f$ at 0. The step function in (d) has a jump. 

Discontinuity : The one-sided limits exist but have different values. The function $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=1/{{\text{x}}^{2}}$ in (e) has an infinite discontinuity. Jumps and infinite discontinuities are the ones most frequently encountered, but there are others. The function in (f) is discontinuous at the origin because it oscillates too much to have a limit as $\text{x}\to 0$.

concepts.title: Types of Discontinuities
concepts.conceptBody:
Types of discontinuities 

(1) Discontinuity of First kind: The point $\text{x}=\text{a}$ will be a point of discontinuity of first type if both right hand and left-hand limits at $\text{x}=\text{a}$ exist but are not equal, i.e.

$\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{h\to 0}}f\left( a-h \right)\ne \text{L}{{\text{t}}_{h\to 0}}f\left( a+h \right)$

(2) Discontinuity of Second kind: The point $\text{x}=$ a will be a point of discontinuity of second type if either or both the right hand and left-hand limits do not exist or if either or both the limits

$\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\text{f}\left( \text{a}-0 \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\underset{\text{h}\to 0}{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\text{f}\left( \text{a}+0 \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ are }\!\!~\!\!\text{ infinite}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

(3) Removable Discontinuity : $\text{L}{{\text{t}}_{h\to 0}}f\left( a+h \right)=\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,f\left( a-h \right)$ i.e., limit exists but is not equal to $\text{f}$ (a) i.e., value, then the function is said to have a removable discontinuity. We can re-define the function at $\text{x}=\text{a}$ so that limit = value and thus the discontinuity has been removed and the function has become continuous now.

concepts.title: Continuity at end points
concepts.conceptBody:
Continuity at end points 

Continuity at end points is defined by taking one-sided limits.

 

Definition 

A function $\text{f}$ is continuous at a left end point $\text{x}=\text{a}$ of its domain if $\underset{x\to {{a}^{+}}}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,f\left( x \right)=f\left( a \right)$

and continuous at a right end point $\text{x}=\text{b}$ of its domain if

$\underset{x\to {{b}^{-}}}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,f\left( x \right)=f\left( b \right)$

In general, a function $\text{f}$ is right-continuous (continuous from the right) at a point $\text{x}=\text{c}$ in its domain if $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to c+}}f\left( x \right)=f\left( c \right)$. It is left-continuous (continuous from the left) at $c$ if $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to c-}}$ $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{f}\left( \text{c} \right)$. Thus, a function is continuous at a left end point a of its domain if it is right-continuous at $a$ and continuous at a right end point $b$ of its domain if it is left-continuous at $b$. A function is continuous at an interior point $\text{c}$ of its domain if and only if it is both right-continuous and left continuous at $c$.

An example of a function which is continuous at every point in domain:-

concepts.title: Continuity Test
concepts.conceptBody:
Continuity Test 

A function $ ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)$ is continuous at $ ext{x}= ext{c}$ if and only if it meets the following three conditions.

  1. $f\left( c ight)$ exists (c lies in the domain of $f$ )
  2. $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{x o c}}f\left( x ight)$ exists (f has a limit as $\left. x o c ight)$
  3. $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{x o c}}f\left( x ight)=f\left( c ight) ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ }$ (The limit equals the function value)

For one-sided continuity and continuity at an end point, the limits in parts 2 and 3 of the test should be replaced by the appropriate one-sided limits.

 

Q) Consider the function $y=f\left( x ight)$ whose domain is the closed interval $\left[ 0,4 ight]$. Discuss the continuity of $f$ at $x=0,1,2,3$ and 4. 

Solution: 

The continuity test gives the following results:

(a) $f$ is continuous at $ ext{x}=0$ because

(i) $ ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ f}\left( 0 ight)$ exists $\left( ext{f}\left( 0 ight)=1 ight)$

(ii) $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{x o 0+}}f\left( x ight)=1$ (the right-hand limit exists at this left end point)

(iii) $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{ ext{x} o 0+}} ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)= ext{f}\left( 0 ight)$ (the limit equals the function value).

(b) $f$ is discontinuous at $x=1$ because $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{x o 1}}f\left( x ight)$ does

not exist. Part 2 of the test fails: f has different right- and left-hand limits at the interior point $ ext{x}=1$. However, $ ext{f}$ is right-continuous at $ ext{x}=1$ because.

(i) $ ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ f}\left( 1 ight)$ exists $\left( ext{f}\left( 1 ight)=1 ight)$,

(ii) $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{ ext{x} o 1+}} ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)=1$ (the right-hand limit exists at $ ext{x}=1$ ),

(iii) $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{x o 1+}}f\left( x ight)=f\left( 1 ight)$ (the right-hand limit equals the function value).

(c) $f$ is discontinuous at $x=2$ because $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{x o 2}}f\left( x ight) e f\left( 2 ight)$. Part 3 of the test fails.

(d) $ ext{f}$ is continuous at $ ext{x}=3$ because

(i) $ ext{f}\left( 3 ight)$ exists (f (3) $=2$ ),

(ii) $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{ ext{x} o 3}} ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight)=2$ (the limit exists at $ ext{x}=2$ ),

(iii) $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{x o 3}}f\left( x ight)=f\left( 3 ight)$ (the limit equals the function value).

(e) $f$ is discontinuous at the right end point $x=4$ because $ ext{li}{{ ext{m}}_{x o 4-}}f\left( x ight) e f\left( 4 ight)$. The right endpoint version of part3 of the test fails.

Rules of Continuity 

If two functions are continuous at a point, then various algebraic combinations of those functions are continuous at that point.

Continuity of Algebraic Combinations 

If functions $ ext{f}$ and $ ext{g}$ are continuous at $ ext{x}= ext{c}$, then the following functions are continuous at $ ext{x}= ext{c}$ :

  1. $ ext{f}+ ext{g}$ and $ ext{f}- ext{g}$
  2. $ ext{fg}$
  3. $ ext{kf}$, where $ ext{k}$ is any number
  4. $ ext{ }\!\!~\!\! ext{ f}/ ext{g}$ (Provided $ ext{g}\left( ext{c} ight) e 0$ )
  5. ${{( ext{f}\left( ext{x} ight))}^{ ext{m}/ ext{n}}}$ (provided $ ext{f}{{( ext{x})}^{ ext{m}/ ext{n}}}$ is defined on an interval containing $ ext{c}$, and $ ext{m}$ and $ ext{n}$ are integers)

As a consequence, polynomials and rational functions are continuous at every point where they are defined.

concepts.title: Continuity of Functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Continuity of Polynomials and Rational Functions 

Every polynomial is continuous at every point of the real line. Every rational function is continuous at every point where its denominator is different from zero.

Example: 

The functions $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{\text{x}}^{4}}+20$ and $\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)=5\text{x}\left( \text{x}-2 \right)$ are continuous at every value of $\text{x}\text{.}$The function

$r\left( x \right)=\frac{f\left( x \right)}{g\left( x \right)}=\frac{{{x}^{4}}+20}{5x\left( x-2 \right)}$

is continuous at every value of $\text{x}$ except $\text{x}=0$ and $\text{x}=2$, where the denominator is 0.

Example: 

Continuity of $f\left( x \right)=\left| x \right|$

The function $f\left( x \right)=\left| x \right|$ is continuous at every value of $x$ (fig. 1.41). If $x>0$, we have

$f\left( x \right)=x$, a polynomial. If $x<0$, we have $f\left( x \right)=-x$, another polynomial. Finally, at

the origin, $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to 0}}\left| x \right|$ $=0=\left| 0 \right|$.

 

Example: 

Continuity of trigonometric functions 

The functions sin $\text{x}$ and $\text{cosx}$ are continuous at every value of $x$. Accordingly, the quotients

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{tan}x=\frac{\text{sin}x}{\text{cos}x} & \text{cot}x=\frac{\text{cos}x}{\text{sin}x} \\ \text{sec}x=\frac{1}{\text{cos}x} & \text{cos}x=\frac{1}{\text{sin}x} \\ \end{array}$

are continuous at every point where they are defined.

 

Note : If $\text{f}$ is continuous at $\text{c}$, and $\text{g}$ is continuous at $\text{f}\left( \text{c} \right)$, then $\text{g}$ o $\text{f}$ is continuous at $\text{c}$ (see Fig.).

The continuity of composites holds for any finite number of functions. The only requirement is that each function be continuous where it is applied.

A function $f$ is said to be continuous on an interval $I$ in its domain if $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{x\to c}}f\left( x \right)=f\left( c \right)$ at every interior point $c$ and if the appropriate one-sided limits equal the function values at any end points I may contain. A function continuous on an interval I is automatically continuous on any interval contained in I. Polynomials are continuous on every interval, and rational functions are continuous on every interval on which they are defined.

Some examples of Functions continuous on intervals:-

  

Functions that are continuous on intervals have properties that make them particularly useful in mathematics and its applications. One of these is the intermediate value property. A function is said to have the intermediate value property if it never takes on two values without taking on all the values in between.

concepts.title: Intermediate Value Theorem
concepts.conceptBody:
The Intermediate Value Theorem 

Suppose $f\left( x \right)$ is continuous on an interval $I$, and a and b are any two points of $I$. Then if ${{y}_{0}}$ is a number between $\text{f}\left( \text{a} \right)$ and $\text{f}\left( \text{b} \right)$, there exists a number $\text{c}$ between a and $\text{b}$ such that $\text{f}\left( \text{c} \right)={{\text{y}}_{0}}.$

The continuity of $\text{f}$ on $\text{I}$ is essential to the theorem. If $\text{f}$ is discontinuous at even one point of $\text{I}$, the theorem's conclusion may fail, as it does for the function graphed in Fig. 2.

concepts.title: Piece wise Function
concepts.conceptBody:
Piece wise Function

Q) The function 

$f(x)=\frac{x^2}{a} \quad$ if $0 \leq x<1$ 

$=a, \quad$ if $1 \leq x<\sqrt{2}$

$=\frac{2 b^2-4 b}{x^2}, \quad$ if $\sqrt{2} \leq x<\infty$ 

is continuous for $0\le \text{x}<\infty $; then find the most suitable values of a and $b$. 

Solution: Given, $\text{f}\left( 1 \right)=\text{a}$

\(\underset{x\to 1-0}{\mathop{\text{Lt f}\left( x \right)}}\,=\underset{x\to 1-0}{\mathop{\text{Lt}}}\,\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{a}=\frac{1}{a}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\underset{{}}{\mathop{\underset{x\to 1+0}{\mathop{\text{Lt }f\left( x \right)}}\,=\underset{x\to a}{\mathop{\text{Lt }}}\,}}\,a=a\)

Given, $f\left( \sqrt{2} \right)=\frac{2{{b}^{2}}-4b}{{{(\sqrt{2})}^{2}}}={{b}^{2}}-2b$

and $\underset{x\to \sqrt{2}+0}{\mathop{\text{Lt f}\left( x \right)=}}\,\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{Lt}\left( a \right)=a & \frac{2{{b}^{2}}-4b}{{{x}^{2}}}={{b}^{2}}-2b \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is continuous in $\left[ 0,\infty \right]$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ it is continuous at $\text{x}=1$ and $\text{x}=\sqrt{2}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{Ltf}\left( x \right)= & \text{Lt}f\left( x \right)=f\left( 1 \right) \\ x\to 1-0 & x\to 1+0 \\ \end{array}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{a}=a\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\therefore a=\pm 1$

And $\underset{x\to \sqrt{2}-0}{\mathop{\operatorname{Lt}f(x)}}\,=\underset{x\to \sqrt{2}+0}{\mathop{\operatorname{Lt}f(x)}}\,=f(\sqrt{2})\text{ or }a={{b}^{2}}-2b$

or ${{b}^{2}}-2b-a=0$

When $\text{a}=1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}^{2}}-2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}-1=0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }b=\frac{2\pm 2\sqrt{2}}{2}=1\pm \sqrt{2}$

When $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ a}=-1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}^{2}}-2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}+1=0,{{(\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}-1)}^{2}}=0$ or $\text{b}=1$

Answer $\text{a}=1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}=1\pm \sqrt{2};\text{a}=-1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}=1$

 

Q) Determine the constants $a,b$, c for which the function 

$f\left( x \right)=\left\{ \begin{matrix}  {{(1+ax)}^{1/x}} & x<0 \\  & x=0 \\ \frac{{{(x+c)}^{1/3}}-1}{{{(x+1)}^{1/2}}-1} & x>0 \\ \end{matrix} \right.$ 

is continuous at $x=0$ 

Solution: 

For function to be continuous at $\text{x}=0$

$\text{RHL}=\text{LHL}=\text{f}\left( 0 \right)\And $

RHL & LHL should be finite

now, solving first LHL at $\text{x}=0$

$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(0-h)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(-h)$

$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(1-h) \frac{-1}{h}$

$=e^a\left\{\right.$ using $\left.\lim _{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \rightarrow 0}(1+\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}))^{1 / \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})}=\mathrm{e}\right\}$

RHL $=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(0+h)$

$=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{(\mathrm{h}+\mathrm{c})^{1 / 3}-1}{(\mathrm{~h}+1)^{1 / 2}-1}$
